dialogue.utterance: Second Medical Unit for cardiac arrest or death call at Tower Block 1944 in Richmond. Patient found unresponsive, overdose suspected.
Hi, I'm Luke, a paramedic with the city squad. What made you call 911 today?
She wasn't breathing. I found her unconscious in the bedroom while her daughter was crying for her.
She's unresponsive. Let me check her pupils. Partner, you see the same?
Pupils are constricted and non-reactive. GCS is very low, right around 3.
Airway is patent with a nasopharyngeal airway in place. Partner, continue BVM ventilation while we monitor her breathing.
Her pulse is 107, strong and regular. No breathing observed, so she's not maintaining her own airway.
Blood pressure is 84 over 54. Alright, but she's not breathing independently.
SpO2 is 99 percent, but still no spontaneous respirations. Need to continue BVM until we stabilize her.
No breathing. Starting BVM with high-flow oxygen.
Capnography is at 58. She's not breathing on her own. Continue BVM until she shows improvement.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. No signs of trauma observed.
Let's set up an IV in an extremity vein. Starting with a 20-gauge access for normal saline.
On EKG, it shows sinus tachycardia. Keep the four-lead monitor on for rhythm strips.
Glucose was initially 103, but it's dropped to zero now. Seen this shift with sedation.
From previous records, she has a known history of opioid abuse. Any known allergies or medications?
She's 24 years old, 68 kg. No apparent needles or paraphernalia on or around her.
She hasn't taken a breath on her own. Initial respiratory rate was 17 while on BVM.
Skin shows no abnormalities, no lesions or rashes. She has no signs of trauma or stab wounds.
Pupillary exam is non-reactive, both eyes. No signs of neck trauma-no jugular distention.
Checked for obstruction-none found. No signs of rhinorrhea, no ear drainage.
Left lung sounds are absent. Right side diminished. Continuing to monitor left's effort.
Abdomen soft and non-tender. No rigidity or distention observed.
No noticeable injury or deformity. No signs of bleeding or trauma on extremities or back.
Administering 0.25 mg of Naloxone IV now. She's on normal saline and still unresponsive.
We're following protocol 7-1 for overdose with opioid. She's on BVM, IV is running, and EKG is monitoring.
Blood pressure is 82 over 40 now, pulse is 105, and respirations are 12. She's still not showing spontaneous respiratory effort.
Vital update: Blood pressure 102/53, pulse is 112, respirations 28, with an SpO2 drop to 98 percent.
Another check: 21 respirations, pulse 125. Her BP has spiked to 197 over 163, and SpO2 is 81.
Scene is cleared. Preparing transport to VCU Health Systems. Rolling her onto the Reeves to move down from the second floor.
She's safely on the stretcher and ready. We'll use a standard sheet move transfer to the hospital bed.

Patient found unresponsive with suspected opioid overdose. Treated per protocol 7-1. IV established, Naloxone administered, on 4-lead EKG, BVM used. Vitals showed a brief increase in BP up to 197/163 with sinus tach.
Blood pressure is 89/44, pulse now at 96 with decrease in tachycardia. SpO2 returned to 100 percent on mask.
We've repeated normal saline IV at 250 mL to maintain perfusion. A responder is also on standby with repeat Naloxone.
She has no pain reported, as expected with current condition. Should stay prioritizing circulation instead.
Opioid abuse documented in prior records. No current meds. Allergic history unclear-bystander unsure.
Still no resolution. She's responding slightly now-her eyes are opening. Yes, I think she's waking up.
She is showing motor response in some extremities, responding appropriately, but very slowly.
Vital update is 95/45 mmHg, pulse 92. SpO2 stable now at 100 percent. No more BVM needed.
IV decreased to keep line open. She started spontaneous breathing-rate now 16 per minute.
On the move to hospital fast. Clearing passengers and making it safe so she doesn't jar or worsen.
She is now breathing on her own, no need for further obstruction support. IV maintained and EKG continues. ED staff are aware.
GCS was 3 and increased to 15 in last two hours, likely due to Naloxone and saline support. Based on field observations.
VCU Health Systems accepting patient. Let's depart. Radio is acknowledging and en route.
Her vitals steady on 95/45, pulse 92. Done recording before exit.
Last dose of Naloxone administered. No side effects noted. Continuing to monitor any reversal complications in ED.
Bed rolled straight to her-secured with full straps. No need to realign mid-ambulance.
We handed over medical history-opioid abuse, no meds, no allergies. ED aware of tachycardia from overdose.
Per EMS prior, she has no medical labor or complicated history. Improved immediately with Naloxone.
Head exam shows reactive pupils again. No abnormal movement in extraocular or oculomotor nerves.
Neck checks clear per scene with no rigidity. She's disoriented in short-term memory, but focused and answers basic questions.
Exam of the back, chest, and extremities shows full motor function-good recovery from initial state.
SpO2 back at 99-on O2 via mask alone. Still no need for BVM.
She reported no pain. No concern about ongoing neural issues from overdose. No organ irritation seen.
ED is preparing. Bystander to accompany so medical discussion doesn't get cut off before detail exchange.
Released patient medical records and signed by her under "First Responder for Recovery." ED aware of them.
Reiterated cause as overdose. Initial call still correct: over-sedation, but recovered to 15 GCS via reversal.
Final BP is 95/45 before arrival. No crashing hemodynamics occurring-yellow light if more than 100 dropping.
VCU accepting her. All protocols are practiced. No more than 10 minutes of travel delay. Scene is clean.
Completed resuscitation and time-sensitive delivery from overdose. No more resuscitative action required now.
Secure stretcher, double secure patient. She is safe for delivery from one bed to another. All right, good to go.
